elements.sent-2411-001: Alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers are precision probes well-suited for tests of fundamental physics and inertial rotation sensing, combining high sensitivity of the spin-exchange-relaxation free (SERF) magnetometers with inherent suppression of magnetic field noise.
elements.sent-2411-002: Past versions of the device utilizing continuous-wave optical pumping are sensitive to a single axis perpendicular to the plane spanned by the orthogonal pump and probe laser beams.
elements.sent-2411-003: These devices are susceptible to light shifts in the alkali atoms, and to power and beam pointing fluctuations of both the probe and pump lasers, the latter of which is a dominant source of \(1/f\) noise.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we model and implement a new approach to alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers using pulsed optical pumping.
elements.sent-2411-005: After each pump laser pulse, an off-resonance probe beam measures the precession of noble-gas-spins-coupled alkali spins via optical rotation in the dark, thus eliminating effects from pump laser light shift and power fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-006: Performing non-linear fitting on the sinusoidal transient signal with a proper phase enables separate and simultaneous measurement of signals along two orthogonal axes in the plane perpendicular to the pump beam.
elements.sent-2411-007: Effects from beam pointing fluctuations of the probe beam in the pump-probe plane is fundamentally eliminated, and signal response to pump beam pointing fluctuations is suppressed by compensation from noble-gas nuclear spins.
elements.sent-2411-008: Searches for new physics at low energy often involve precision measurement of anomalous spin-dependent interactions \cite{safronova18 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Many comagnetometer systems have been demonstrated to be well-suited for this purpose \cite{terrano21 , relying on the differentiation of systematic effects from signal of interest made possible by measuring common systematic effects with one spin ensemble, therefore revealing weak signal in the other.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Different interactions under investigation command different combinations of spin species, for instance, two electron-spin species for electron electric dipole moment (EDM) searches \cite{weisskopf68,Chupp19 , two nuclear-spin species for \(^{129}\)Xe EDM measurements \cite{Rosenberry01 and tests of Lorentz invariance \cite{lamoreaux86 and CPT symmetry \cite{bear00 , or a pair of electron-spin and nuclear-spin species for test of Lorentz invariance \cite{berglund95 and axion-like coupling searches \cite{youdin96 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The two ensembles may also consist of the same species in a spatially non-overlapping configuration \cite{lamoreaux87 , in a Bose-Einstein condensate with two distinct hyperfine manifolds \cite{Gomez20 , or in an ensemble of molecular species where different nuclear spins in the same molecule are compared \cite{wu18 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The state of the art of such systems is set by spin-exchange relaxation free (SERF) atomic magnetometers \cite{allred02,kominis03,dang10 , which takes advantage of the vanishing of spin-exchange broadening at high alkali-metal vapor density and in near-zero magnetic field \cite{happer73,happer77 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This work focuses on one type of alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometer with the SERF magnetometer incorporated into its setup \cite{kornack02 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The alkali-metal atoms both polarize noble-gas atoms via spin-exchange optical pumping \cite{walker97,bouchiat60 and precision probe their precession via spin-exchange interaction \cite{schaefer89 , while the noble-gas atoms suppress sensitivity to magnetic field drifts, gradients and transients \cite{kornackThesis,kornack05 , but retain sensitivity to anomalous magnetic-like fields.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Such self-compensating comagnetometers have found application in inertial rotation sensing \cite{kornack05, li16 , as a quantum interface \cite{QI , as well as in fundamental physics, including tests of Lorentz and CPT violation \cite{smiciklas11 , searches for anomalous spin-mass \cite{lee18 and spin-spin \cite{almasi20 interactions, and direct detection of dark matter \cite{lee23 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In the initial K-\(^3\)He comagnetometer, unlike how magnetic fields are suppressed by coupled spin dynamics, light shifts \cite{mathur68 and optical misalignment can be zeroed with an elaborate zeroing procedure \cite{kornackThesis , but not fundamentally suppressed, since they couple only to alkali spins.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Their drifts over time contribute to \(1/f\) noise, whose presence requires all fundamental physics searches to adopt some form of modulation, either of the source or of the whole experiment.
elements.sent-2411-018: A subsequent K-\(^3\)He comagnetometer improved low-frequency performance and achieved the lowest spin energy resolution, corresponding to a magnetic field value less than an aT (10\(^{-14}\) G), by quickly reversing the source spin and modifying pump optics and heating scheme to minimize perturbation of the experiment \cite{vasilakis09 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The next version further shortened optical path length, enclosed all optics in an evacuated bell jar, and periodically reversed the whole setup, which resulted in better \(1/f\) noise suppression \cite{brown10 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In spite of the improvements, fundamental suppression of light shifts and optical misalignment was still lacking.
elements.sent-2411-021: Later adoption of hybrid spin-exchange optical pumping \cite{babcock03 , where optically-pumped low-density Rb vapor transfers polarization to collocating high-density K vapor via spin-exchange collisions \cite{lee18 , reduced polarization gradient in the latter and thus optimized sensitivity, but also introduced a light shift that can no longer be zeroed due to the circularly-polarized pump laser detuned from the K's resonance frequencies.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Replacing K with Cs to hybrid pump Rb \cite{almasi20,jiang18 in a Rb-\(^{21}\)Ne comagnetometer ameliorated the problem by increasing the detuning, and separate studies reduced this light shift by compensation with either light shift in K \cite{chen16 or magnetic field \cite{jiang17 in the K-Rb-\(^{21}\)Ne system, or exploited it to sense dual-axis rotations \cite{li16 , whilst the traditional steady-state DC-signal comagnetometers are sensitive to a single axis.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Both schemes demonstrated improved \(1/f\) noise suppression compared to the DC-mode of operation, although their short-term sensitivities were worse due to magnetic noise introduced by the modulations.
elements.sent-2411-024: Noticeably the latter adopted a single-beam configuration, showing promise for miniaturization.
elements.sent-2411-025: By turning off the pump laser during measurement, pump laser light shift and power fluctuations afflicting DC-mode comagnetometers can be fundamentally eliminated or suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-026: Performing non-linear fitting on the oscillating transient signal enables separation of beam misalignment from real signal, thereby eliminating one dominant source of \(1/f\) noise from beam pointing fluctuations to allow for expansion of comagnetometer bandwidth towards lower frequency range.
elements.sent-2411-027: Here we present results from both experiment and Bloch equation model to demonstrate the above advantages of the pulsed comagnetometer.
elements.sent-2411-028: Experimentally, the elimination of \(1/f\) noise is not yet realized, and we discuss possible limitation on our setup.
elements.sent-2411-029: Overview For the pulsed comagnetometer, we chose \(^{87}\)Rb's electron spins and \(^{21}\)Ne's nuclear spins as the two sensing ensembles due to \(^{21}\)Ne's smaller gyromagnetic ratio, which improves fundamental sensitivity, and Rb's larger spin-exchange cross section with \(^{21}\)Ne \cite{Ghosh10 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Enriched \(^{87}\)Rb is favored over Rb in natural abundance for simplified spin dynamics with only one type of nuclear spin (\(I=3/2\)).
elements.sent-2411-031: Optical pumping polarizes \(^{87}\)Rb atoms, which then polarize \(^{21}\)Ne via spin-exchange collisions.
elements.sent-2411-032: The circularly polarized pump laser along \(\hat{z}\)-axis is tuned to Rb's D1 resonance, and cycles between 6 ms of pumping time, where \(^{87}\)Rb builds up high longitudinal polarization, and 20 ms of dark time, where the polarization precesses and decays.
elements.sent-2411-033: A detuned probe laser along \(\hat{x}\)-axis continuously monitors the spins' \(\hat{x}\)-projection via optical rotation.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the presence of transverse coupling to either \(^{87}\)Rb, \(^{21}\)Ne, or both, the motion of \(^{87}\)Rb's electron spins is a transient hybrid response coupled to \(^{21}\)Ne nuclear spins via spin-exchange, resembling Larmor precession, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:RawSignal (a).}
elements.sent-2411-035: The spin-exchange coupling between the two species due to Fermi-contact interaction can be represented as effective fields \(B^n = \lambda M^{n}\) and \(B^e = \lambda M^{e}\) acting on one another, where \(M^{e}\) and \(M^{n}\) are the magnetizations of electron and nuclear spins.
elements.sent-2411-036: For a spherical cell \(\lambda = 8\pi \kappa_{0 /3\), and the enhancement factor \(\kappa_{0 = 35.7\) for Rb-Ne \cite{Ghosh10 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Typically \(B^n\) is a few mGs and \(B^e\) a few hundred \(\mu\)Gs.
elements.sent-2411-038: Information on transverse spin interactions is contained in the amplitude of the decaying oscillations, which is extracted by fitting the signal to \begin{equation}\label{eqn:fittingForm} S(t)= [Asin(2\pi ft+\phi_0)+Bcos(2\pi ft+\phi_0)]e^{-t/T_2}\nonumber +Ce^{(-t/T_1)}+D,\end{equation} where \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) denote longitudinal and transverse relaxation times respectively, and \(\phi_0\) is a fixed parameter determined from a zeroing procedure described later.

An analog switch blocks photodiode signal during pumping pulse to avoid overloading the lock-in amplifier. The fit starts at 11 ms to avoid the faster-drifting part of oscillation frequency due to polarization-dependent slowing down factor \(Q(P^e)\). Right scale: Pump laser power. Power fluctuations during the pulse result from quick ramp-up of amplifier current. (b) Numerically simulated electron spin \(\hat{x}\)-polarization at compensation point \(B_z=B^c=-(B^n+B^e)\). The various curves represent signal response to \(10^{-7}\) G of \(B_x\), \(\beta^n_{x}\), \(\beta^n_{y}\), or \(\beta^e_{y}\) field, and to a rotation \(\Omega_y=0.2\) mrad/s, corresponding to a \(10^{-7}\) G effective field. elements.sent-2411-039: The pulsed comagnetometer exhibits multiple advantages compared to past versions of alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers operating in DC-mode, while maintaining their key feature of suppressed response to magnetic field, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:SuppDiagram (c).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=8.5cm, keepaspectratio]{SuppDiagram2.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:SuppDiagram} Basic operation of the comagnetometer. (a) Electron spins and noble-gas nuclear spins align with applied bias field \(B^c=-(B^n+B^e)\). Each species generates an effective field felt by the other species indicated by the blue arrows, and feels a field indicated by the green arrows. (b) Deflected pump laser polarizes electron spins along tilted direction, generating a tilted \(B^e\). Noble-gas nuclear spins feel a tilted field \(B^e+B^c\) and adiabatically follow it, generating a tilted \(B^n\). Electron spins feel the field \(B^n+B^c\), which largely aligns with tilted pump laser direction. When pump laser is turned off the electron spins' precession is suppressed as the spins are aligned with the field they feel, and probe laser picks up an exponential decay due to \(T_1\)-relaxation if the deflection results in non-orthogonal pump and probe directions. (c) Noble gas nuclear spins adiabatically follow change in external field, generating a tilted \(B^n\), whose transverse component cancels transverse component of external field for electron spins. When pump laser is turned off the electron spins' precession is suppressed as the spins are aligned with the field they feel, and their projection along probe laser remains zero.}
elements.sent-2411-040: First, the pulsed comagnetometer has dual-axis sensitivity in the transverse plane because any transverse coupling induces electron spin precession, whose \(\hat{x}\)-projection exhibits oscillatory behavior.
elements.sent-2411-041: By comparison, in the DC-mode transverse \(\hat{x}\)-couplings are not detected because they tilt steady-state electron spins into \(\hat{y}\)-axis, causing no change in their \(\hat{x}\)-projection.
elements.sent-2411-042: Second, any \(\hat{z}\)-axis projection of the probe laser shows up as an exponential decay in signal, since the probe picks up a portion of longitudinal electron polarization with \(T_1\)-relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-043: This can be easily differentiated from oscillations, whereas such misalignment shows up as a DC-offset in DC-mode, indistinguishable from transverse couplings of interest.
elements.sent-2411-044: Third, light shift from pump laser is fundamentally eliminated in the pulsed scheme, as the pump laser is not present when measurements are taken.
elements.sent-2411-045: Fourth, the pulsed comagnetometer has suppressed sensitivity to pump laser power fluctuations at sufficiently high pumping rate \(R_p\).
elements.sent-2411-046: While the DC-mode magnetometer's sensitivity is maximized at 50\(\%\) electron spin polarization, the pulsed case's sensitivity increases with equilibrium electron spin polarization when pump laser is on, and its \(R_p\)-dependence asymptotically approaches zero with increasing \(R_p\).
elements.sent-2411-047: Aiming for maximum electron spin polarization during the pulse also means the pump beam burns through the cell causing no polarization gradient (with well-collimated pump beam illuminating all spins).
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus hybrid pumping is not necessary, and spin dynamics can be simplified.
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, re-orientation of noble-gas nuclear spins in response to tilted electron spin polarization due to pump beam deflections suppresses oscillation amplitude of electron spins during dark time, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:SuppDiagram (b).}
elements.sent-2411-050: In DC-mode a tilted pump beam non-orthogonal to probe beam results in a DC-offset indistinguishable from signal of interest.
elements.sent-2411-051: Experimental Apparatus Our experimental apparatus is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Setup , modified from the one used in \cite{smiciklas11 .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The pump beam is generated by a master-oscillator tapered power-amplifier setup with a DBR diode as seed laser, and then expanded to illuminate the whole cell, providing close to 800 mW of pumping power.
elements.sent-2411-053: The pulses are switched on-and-off with an acoustic-optic modulator (AOM) in combination with laser amplifier current modulation to minimize light leakage during dark time.
elements.sent-2411-054: Two beam direction deviators are placed at specific distances, determined with ABCD-matrix calculations, in relation to the lenses for beam-expansion and to each other, such that each separately controls the pump beam's tilt and translation at the cell.
elements.sent-2411-055: Probe laser optics follow a standard polarimetry set up for optical rotation using a photoelastic modulator (PEM), with an added stress plate for cancelling any stray birefringence.
elements.sent-2411-056: Photodiode output is demodulated with a lock-in amplifier and then recorded by a computer that performs non-linear fitting of the signal in real time.
elements.sent-2411-057: The probe beam is linearly polarized, with a diameter of 2 mm and a power of 2 mW.
elements.sent-2411-058: Multiple position detectors and photodiodes monitor laser beam power, position, and angle.
elements.sent-2411-059: All optics are enclosed in a bell jar evacuated to low vacuum to eliminate noise from air currents.
elements.sent-2411-060: The bell jar is wrapped in insulation material to avoid coupling to environmental temperature changes.
elements.sent-2411-061: The whole experiment sits on a rotary air bearing table so its orientation can be rotated around the vertical axis.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=8.6cm, keepaspectratio]{SetupFinal.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:Setup} Schematic diagram of the apparatus. Cell size is exaggerated. Probe optics are mounted upside down on the lower deck. Windows for optical access into the inner vacuum chamber, which is pumped to higher vacuum than inside the bell jar for thermal stability, are slightly tilted to prevent the formation of standing waves. The pump seed laser, tapered amplifier, probe laser, and case of AOM are water-cooled to 18 $^\circ$C on the same line.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The comagnetometer cell contains enriched \(^{87}\)Rb, around 5 atmosphere of \(^{21}\)Ne at room temperature, and 200 torr of N\(_{2}\) for quenching.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is heated to 170 \(^\circ\)C, at which Rb's number density is about 2.6\(\times10^{14}\) cm\(^{-3}\).
elements.sent-2411-064: The hand-blown glass cell is 0.8 cm in diameter and 0.8 cm tall.
elements.sent-2411-065: Its geometry resembles a champagne glass with flat top window to minimize pump-beam distortion, while the curved wall of a traditional spherical cell would act as a thin lens.
elements.sent-2411-066: The cell's top edge is intentionally rounded during fabrication to reduce Ne's dipolar field, whose gradient causes spin-relaxation \cite{Cates88 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:NeDipolar , magnetic field gradient is largest at the top and bottom edges of a cylinder with uniform longitudinal magnetization pointing along positive \(\hat{z -\)axis.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Gradient in the plane of the cylinder's top or bottom surface is mainly radial, and thus primarily reduces Ne \(T_1\).
elements.sent-2411-069: Ne’s dipolar field gradient should not have any significant effect other than shortening Ne’s lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-070: One can still use the geometric factor \(\lambda = 8\pi \kappa_{0 /3\) for spherical geometry because the local contact spin interaction between Rb electron spin and Ne nuclear spin is much larger than Ne’s long-range dipolar field, i.e. the enhancement factor \(\kappa_0\) is 35.7 for the former and 1 for the latter.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Average longitudinal field within a cylinder deviates from the uniform field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere by at most 3\(\%\), and the actual difference is even smaller since the actual cell’s geometry sits somewhere in between a cylinder and a sphere.
elements.sent-2411-072: Optimally the probe beam should horizontally pass through the center of the cell, where Ne's dipolar field is most uniform and transverse field strength is minimal.
elements.sent-2411-073: This also minimizes sensitivity to probe beam translation due to cell dichroism.

elements.sent-2411-220: Conclusion We devise and realize an alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometer with pulsed optical pumping, which improves upon previous generations of DC-mode alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers, while inheriting their inherent suppression of magnetic fields and high sensitivity from being in the near-SERF regime.
elements.sent-2411-221: The pulsed comagnetometer has dual-axis sensitivity, avoids pump laser light shifts by measuring in the dark, suppresses perturbation from pump an probe beam pointing fluctuations, and is insensitive to pump power fluctuations at sufficiently high pumping rate.
elements.sent-2411-222: We also derive an analytic model and describe unique features of the comagnetometer operation in the pulsed regime.
elements.sent-2411-223: A zeroing procedure is designed to minimize the remaining systematic effects, while concurrently calibrating the comagnetometer.
elements.sent-2411-224: With the above advantages, the pulsed comagnetometer exhibits good short-term sensitivity, and we anticipate improved \(1/f\) noise performance since it suppresses beam pointing fluctuations, a major source of \(1/f\) noise.
elements.sent-2411-225: Currently we are limited by defects in cell-fabrication that cause pump beam distortions, particularly of the collimation.
elements.sent-2411-226: Using anodically bonded cell with optimized wall curvature would allow more precise laser beam collimation and increase Ne's equilibrium polarization.

elements.sent-2411-228: Alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers are precision probes well-suited for tests of fundamental physics and inertial rotation sensing, combining the high sensitivity of the spin-exchange-relaxation free (SERF) magnetometers with inherent suppression of magnetic field noise.
elements.sent-2411-229: Past versions of the device utilizing continuous-wave optical pumping are sensitive to rotation and anomalous spin couplings along a single axis perpendicular to the plane spanned by the orthogonal pump and probe laser beams.
elements.sent-2411-230: In this work, we model and implement an approach to alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers using pulsed optical pumping.
elements.sent-2411-231: Performing non-linear fitting on the sinusoidal transient signal with a proper phase enables independent and simultaneous measurement of signals along two orthogonal axes in the plane perpendicular to the pump beam.
elements.sent-2411-232: Effects from beam pointing fluctuations of the probe beam in the pump-probe plane are fundamentally eliminated, and signal response to pump beam pointing fluctuations is suppressed by compensation from noble-gas nuclear spins.

elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=8.5cm, keepaspectratio]{SuppDiagramEnlarged.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:SuppDiagram} Basic operation of the comagnetometer. (a) Electron spins and noble-gas nuclear spins align with the applied bias field \(B^c=-(B^n+B^e)\). Each species generates an effective field felt by the other species indicated by the blue arrows, and feels a field indicated by the green arrows. (b) Deflected pump laser polarizes electron spins along the tilted direction, generating a tilted \(B^e\). Noble-gas nuclear spins feel a tilted field \(B^e+B^c\) and adiabatically follow it, generating a tilted \(B^n\). Electron spins feel the field \(B^n+B^c\), which largely aligns with tilted pump laser direction. When the pump laser is turned off the electron spins' precession is suppressed as the spins are aligned with the field they feel, and the probe laser picks up an exponential decay due to \(T_1\)-relaxation if the deflection results in non-orthogonal pump and probe directions. (c) Noble gas nuclear spins adiabatically follow the change in external field, generating a tilted \(B^n\), whose transverse component cancels the transverse component of external field for electron spins. When the pump laser is turned off the electron spins' precession is suppressed as the spins are aligned with the field they feel, and their projection along the probe laser remains zero.}

elements.sent-2411-294: Conclusion We devise and realize a self-compensating alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometer with pulsed optical pumping, which improves upon previous generations of DC-mode self-compensating alkali-metal-noble-gas comagnetometers, while inheriting their inherent suppression of magnetic fields and high sensitivity from being in the near-SERF regime.
elements.sent-2411-295: The pulsed comagnetometer has dual-axis sensitivity, avoids pump laser light shifts by measuring in the dark, suppresses perturbation from pump and probe beam pointing fluctuations, and is insensitive to pump power fluctuations at sufficiently high pumping rate.
elements.sent-2411-296: With the above advantages, the pulsed comagnetometer exhibits good short-term sensitivity.
